problem_summary: 2022 MCM Problem C (data): given five years of daily LBMA gold prices and BCHAIN bitcoin prices, build a model that decides each day, using only price data up to that day, how to trade $1,000 of cash/gold/bitcoin to maximize total final value while respecting transaction commissions (gold 1%, bitcoin 2%; gold only trades on market-open days). Required deliverables: (1) a price-aware daily trading strategy and its final portfolio value, (2) evidence the strategy is "best," (3) sensitivity of the strategy to the commission rates, (4) a one-to-two-page memorandum to the trader. This team's strategy turned $1,000 into $14,325.13 over the 5 years (~13x, ARR 266.36%).
models_used: LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory) recurrent neural network for daily price forecasting of gold and bitcoin, with full forget/input/output-gate equations written out
Sliding Window Algorithm to frame the time series as supervised learning (predict day t from previous d=10 days; train on d'=5-day sub-windows)
Manual grid/one-at-a-time hyperparameter tuning over hidden size l, window d, training window d', learning rate lambda, epochs epsilon, scored by MAPE

Multi-objective Linear Programming (MLP) portfolio optimizer that simultaneously maximizes predicted return P and minimizes risk R, decision variables Delta x_g and Delta x_b (dollars moved into/out of gold and bitcoin)

Ideal Point Method to scalarize the two objectives: solve each single-objective problem for its optimum omega_i, then minimize the sum of squared deviations sum (f_i - omega_i)^2 as a quadratic program

Case-split formulation: six separate LP models for the six trading behaviors (buy/sell combinations of gold+bitcoin, plus bitcoin-only buy/sell on gold non-trading days), each with commission-correct constraints
Rolling-window volatility (10-day sample standard deviation S_g, S_b) used as the risk coefficient in the objective
Granville's eight moving-average rules as a benchmark 'Rule Asset' trading strategy
Buy-and-hold gold, buy-and-hold bitcoin, and a perfect-foresight 'Ideal Asset' (same MLP fed true future prices) as additional benchmarks
key_techniques: Two-stage decomposition: forecast first (LSTM), then optimize the trade given the forecast (MLP) — cleanly separates prediction error from decision logic

Commission-correct algebra: each of the six cases substitutes x'-x in terms of (1-alpha)*Delta x for buys and Delta x/(1-alpha) for sells, so the LP objective and constraints carry the real transaction cost
A 'do nothing' guard (eq. 13): even the LP-optimal trade is executed only if its predicted next-day worth beats holding the current portfolio, suppressing churn that commissions would eat
Risk modeled as a concrete, computable quantity (recent 10-day price volatility scaled by dollars moved), not left abstract
MAPE as the single tuning/selection metric for forecasts (gold 0.00726, bitcoin 0.04163)
10-metric evaluation battery for Task 2: ARR, Profit/Loss Ratio, Number of Trades, Win Rate, Max Profit/Loss Amount, Max Consecutive Profits/Losses, Maximum Drawdown, Sharpe Ratio
Validating the Sharpe-ratio precondition by plotting the daily-return distribution of each strategy and arguing approximate normality before computing SR

Sensitivity done as controlled experiments: vary one commission across a grid (gold 0-4%, bitcoin 1-5%) holding the other fixed, and report both behavior metrics (investment percentages, trade counts) and outcome metrics (net profit, win rate)
Honest benchmarking against a perfect-foresight upper bound (Ideal Asset, ARR 1406%, SR 0.2955) to show how much of the achievable value the real strategy captured
why_it_won: From a judge's view this paper wins on decision-model rigor and disciplined validation rather than on a flashy forecaster. The standout is the MLP/ideal-point optimizer with six commission-exact LP cases plus the explicit no-trade guard — it directly answers the actual question (what to do given a forecast and real frictions) instead of stopping at 'predict the price.' Task 2 is unusually strong: ten named metrics, four genuinely different benchmarks (buy-and-hold gold, buy-and-hold bitcoin, Granville rule-based, and a perfect-foresight ideal), and the key honest framing that their strategy is second only to the impossible clairvoyant strategy on Sharpe ratio and far more risk-resilient than all-in bitcoin (MDD 42.9% vs 83.4%). Judges reward a team that ranks itself against an upper bound and admits the gap. Sensitivity analysis separates effect-on-behavior from effect-on-outcome and reaches a crisp, defensible conclusion (strategy is more sensitive to bitcoin commission). The risk quantity is concrete and the commission algebra is auditable. Together these make the work reproducible and the claims falsifiable — exactly what an O-award rubric rewards.
section_highlights: Tightly mirrors the four COMAP tasks one-to-one, with a Figure 1 master flowchart up front. Assumptions are justified with outside facts (e.g., looked up the LBMA 100-troy-ounce minimum lot to justify the no-minimum-purchase assumption). The LSTM section derives all gate equations (Sigmoid/tanh forget-input-output) and shows a hyperparameter sweep table with MAPE per setting. The MLP section is the spine: general multi-objective form, ideal-point scalarization, then six fully written LP sub-models with behavior-specific constraints. Task 2 packs a single comparison table (Table 4) of all 5 strategies x 10 metrics plus per-strategy daily-return distribution plots to license the Sharpe ratio. Task 3 uses paired tables (behavior metrics, then performance metrics) for each commission sweep. The memorandum is correctly formatted (To/From/Date/Subject) with three plain-language actionable recommendations and an explicit statement of model limits. Closes with a per-model Strengths/Weaknesses section. Notation table and keyword list are present.
pitfalls_or_limitations: Forecast leakage risk / over-optimistic prediction: gold MAPE of 0.0073 on a noisy 5-year daily series is suspiciously low; the paper does not clearly state a strict walk-forward train/test split or how the LSTM is retrained as the window rolls, so the reported accuracy may overstate true out-of-sample performance — and the whole strategy result rides on it

Single hyperparameter combo assumed optimal for BOTH assets (explicit assumption), and tuning is one-factor-at-a-time, which cannot find interacting optima; LSTM stochasticity is acknowledged but no seed averaging / confidence interval is given

The figure data tables in the markdown are clearly OCR/extraction artifacts (round placeholder numbers), but even in the original the asset-curve evidence is visual; few statistical significance tests beyond Sharpe

Risk = 10-day rolling standard deviation is a fixed, untuned window (self-identified weakness); no covariance/correlation between gold and bitcoin is modeled — assets assumed independent, which is a strong simplification

'Proof of best strategy' is overstated: it is best among 5 chosen benchmarks on selected metrics, not optimal in any formal sense; on raw ARR plain buy-and-hold bitcoin (906%) beat their strategy (266%) — they reframe the win around risk-adjusted return (Sharpe/MDD), which is reasonable but should be stated as a tradeoff not a dominance

Inconsistent date/window reporting: body uses 10-day gold window from Sept 11 2016; memorandum says 'previous 14 days' and a different date range (Nov 9 2016 - Oct 9 2021) — sloppiness a judge could notice

Calling the optimizer 'MLP' invites confusion with multilayer perceptron; it is multi-objective linear programming. Also the ideal-point step yields a quadratic (not linear) program, so 'linear programming' is loosely used

Commission constraints use strict inequalities and continuous variables with no transaction lot sizing beyond the no-minimum assumption; real fill/slippage ignored (single daily price assumed for buy and sell)
reusable_patterns: Forecast-then-optimize two-stage architecture: keep the predictor (LSTM/ARIMA/Prophet) decoupled from a decision optimizer, so each can be validated and swapped independently — broadly reusable for any sequential decision-under-uncertainty data problem
Ideal-point method to collapse a maximize-return/minimize-risk multi-objective into one solvable program — a clean, citable alternative to hand-tuned weighting

Embed real-world frictions (commissions) directly in the constraint algebra via (1-alpha) buy / 1/(1-alpha) sell substitutions, and add an explicit do-nothing baseline comparison to prevent over-trading

Case-split optimization: when the action space has qualitatively different regimes (buy/sell/hold per asset, trading vs non-trading days), write one constrained sub-model per regime and pick the best feasible one

Multi-metric scorecard + multi-benchmark table as the standard 'is my model good?' deliverable: include naive baselines, a domain-standard baseline (Granville), AND a perfect-information upper bound so the reader sees both floor and ceiling
Validate a metric's assumptions before using it (plot return distribution to justify Sharpe ratio normality) — shows statistical maturity cheaply
Sensitivity analysis that reports behavior-metrics and outcome-metrics separately over a one-variable-at-a-time grid, then narrates the mechanism (why the strategy shifts), not just the numbers
Justify modeling assumptions with concrete external facts (e.g., exchange minimum lot sizes) rather than asserting them
Per-model Strengths/Weaknesses self-critique section and a correctly-formatted memorandum with 3 plain-English action items are low-cost structural moves that align directly with the rubric
